question_text: Not-for-Profit Organisations are established primarily for:
explanation: Not-for-Profit Organisations (NPOs) such as clubs, hospitals, schools, and charitable trusts are established to provide services to members or the public. Their primary objective is service, NOT profit-making.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Providing service to members or society

elimination_hints.hint: Profit-making is the objective of business entities, not NPOs.
NPOs have no shareholders — they have members.
Trading is a commercial activity. NPOs focus on welfare/service.

question_text: Receipts and Payments Account is a summary of:
explanation: Receipts and Payments Account is a summary of all cash and bank transactions during the accounting period. It is prepared from the Cash Book and records ALL receipts (debits) and ALL payments (credits) — both capital and revenue.
subject: accountancy

options.text: All cash and bank transactions during the year

elimination_hints.hint: R&P includes BOTH revenue and capital items, not just revenue.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking R&P is limited to revenue items.

elimination_hints.hint: Capital items are included but R&P also records revenue transactions.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking R&P is limited to capital items.

elimination_hints.hint: Accrued items are non-cash and belong to the Income & Expenditure Account, not R&P.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing cash basis with accrual basis.

question_text: Receipts and Payments Account starts with:
explanation: The Receipts and Payments Account starts with the opening balance of Cash and Bank (debit side). This is because it is essentially a summarised Cash Book — the opening cash/bank balance is the first entry on the receipts (debit) side.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Opening balance of Cash and Bank

elimination_hints.hint: Capital Fund appears on the Balance Sheet, not in the R&P Account.
Surplus/Deficit is the result of the Income & Expenditure Account, not R&P.
Subscriptions are recorded during the year but the account starts with opening cash/bank balance.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing a receipt item with opening balance.

question_text: Receipts and Payments Account ends with:
explanation: The R&P Account ends with the closing balance of Cash and Bank. The total of the receipts side minus total payments gives the closing cash/bank balance (always a debit balance — you cannot pay more cash than you have).
subject: accountancy

options.text: Closing balance of Cash and Bank

elimination_hints.hint: Net Income (Surplus) is determined from the I&E Account, not R&P.
Capital Fund is a Balance Sheet item.
Surplus/Deficit comes from I&E Account. R&P only shows cash position.

question_text: Statement: Receipts and Payments Account records only cash transactions (actual receipts and actual payments).
explanation: This is TRUE. The R&P Account is prepared on a CASH BASIS. It records only actual cash/bank receipts and payments — no outstanding, prepaid, or accrued items. It is like a summarised Cash Book.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: R&P is a cash-basis statement. Accrual adjustments are done in the I&E Account.

question_text: Which of the following appears on the RECEIPTS (debit) side of the Receipts and Payments Account?
explanation: Subscriptions received from members is a receipt and appears on the debit side of the R&P Account. Salaries paid, rent paid, and stationery purchased are all payments that appear on the credit (payments) side.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Salaries paid is a payment — it goes on the credit (payments) side.
Rent paid is a payment (outflow), not a receipt.
Stationery purchased is a cash outflow — payments side.

question_text: Which of the following is a CAPITAL receipt in the Receipts and Payments Account?
explanation: Sale of old furniture is a capital receipt because it involves disposal of a fixed asset. Subscriptions (revenue receipt), entrance fees (can be capital or revenue — but per NCERT, treated as revenue), and locker rent are revenue receipts.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Subscriptions are periodic revenue receipts from members.
As per NCERT, entrance fees of small amounts are treated as revenue income.
elimination_hints.misconception: Classifying entrance fees as always capital.

elimination_hints.hint: Locker rent is recurring revenue income.

question_text: Receipts and Payments Account does NOT show:
explanation: The R&P Account does NOT show depreciation because depreciation is a non-cash item. R&P only records actual cash transactions. Depreciation is an adjustment made in the Income & Expenditure Account.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Life membership fees is an actual cash receipt — it appears in R&P.
Donation received is actual cash inflow — recorded in R&P.
Tournament fund receipts are actual cash — they appear in R&P.

question_text:
Assertion (A): Receipts and Payments Account includes transactions of both current and previous/next years.
Reason (R): R&P Account is prepared on cash basis — it records all cash received and paid during the year regardless of the period to which they relate.
explanation: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A. Since R&P is cash-based, it records subscriptions received for previous, current, and next years if actually received during the current year. Similarly, payments made for prior-year dues or advance payments are included.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains WHY multi-period items appear — because it's cash-based.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not linking cash basis with multi-period recording.

elimination_hints.hint: R is factually true — R&P IS prepared on cash basis.
A is correct — subscriptions for next year received now DO appear in R&P.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking R&P only records current-year items.

question_text: Receipts and Payments Account is equivalent to which book in a trading organisation?
explanation: The Receipts and Payments Account of an NPO is equivalent to the Cash Book of a trading organisation. Both record all cash and bank transactions. The difference is that R&P is a summarised version prepared at the end of the year.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: P&L shows profit — the NPO equivalent is Income & Expenditure Account, not R&P.
Trading Account shows cost of goods sold — NPOs don't trade.
Balance Sheet shows financial position — R&P only shows cash flows.

question_text: The excess of income over expenditure in an NPO is called ______, not profit.
explanation: In an NPO, the excess of income over expenditure is called SURPLUS (not profit). Conversely, excess of expenditure over income is called DEFICIT (not loss). This terminology reflects that NPOs are not profit-driven.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Revenue is income, not the excess of income over expenditure.
Dividends are distributed by companies, not NPOs.
Gross Profit is a trading concept. NPOs use 'Surplus'.

question_text: Which of the following is NOT a feature of the Receipts and Payments Account?
explanation: The R&P Account does NOT distinguish between capital and revenue items — it records ALL cash transactions together. This distinction is made in the Income & Expenditure Account and Balance Sheet. R&P simply summarises cash inflows and outflows.
subject: accountancy

options.text: It is a real account (debit what comes in, credit what goes out)
It starts with opening cash/bank balance
It records both capital and revenue items
It distinguishes between capital and revenue items separately

elimination_hints.hint: R&P is indeed a real account — this IS a correct feature.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking R&P is not a real account.

elimination_hints.hint: Starting with opening balance IS a feature of R&P.
R&P does record both types — but it doesn't separate them.

question_text: Which of the following is an example of a Not-for-Profit Organisation?
explanation: A sports club is a classic example of a Not-for-Profit Organisation. It exists to serve its members, not to earn profit. Other examples include hospitals, schools, libraries, charitable trusts, and religious institutions.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Reliance is a for-profit corporation listed on stock exchanges.
SBI is a banking corporation that earns profit.
Flipkart is a private company operating for profit.

question_text: Opening balance of Cash ₹15,000; Bank ₹85,000. Total receipts during the year ₹4,50,000. Total payments during the year ₹3,80,000. The closing balance of Cash and Bank is:
explanation: Opening balance = ₹15,000 + ₹85,000 = ₹1,00,000. Closing balance = Opening + Receipts – Payments = ₹1,00,000 + ₹4,50,000 – ₹3,80,000 = ₹1,70,000.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹70,000 = ₹4,50,000 – ₹3,80,000. You forgot to add the opening balance.
₹1,00,000 is the opening balance, not closing.
₹5,50,000 = ₹1L + ₹4.5L. You added receipts but forgot to deduct payments.

question_text: Statement: The Receipts and Payments Account can show a credit balance (i.e., payments exceeding receipts plus opening balance).
explanation: This is FALSE. The R&P Account can NEVER show a credit balance because you cannot pay out more cash than you have. The closing balance (cash/bank in hand) is always zero or positive (debit). If there is a bank overdraft, it appears as a liability on the Balance Sheet.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: You can't pay more cash than you have. R&P closing balance is always debit (positive cash).
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking R&P can have credit balance.

question_text: Legacy received by an NPO is a:
explanation: Legacy is a gift received under a will (after someone's death). It is a capital receipt because it is non-recurring and of significant amount. It is generally capitalised and shown on the Balance Sheet, not in the Income & Expenditure Account.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Legacy is non-recurring and large — it is capital, not revenue.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating one-time large receipts as revenue.

elimination_hints.hint: Legacy is a receipt (inflow), not an expenditure (outflow).
Capital expenditure means spending on assets. Legacy is a receipt.

question_text: Life Membership Fees received by a club should be:
explanation: Life Membership Fees is a one-time payment that covers membership for the entire lifetime of the member. It is a CAPITAL receipt and is directly added to the Capital Fund on the Balance Sheet. It is NOT shown in the Income & Expenditure Account as income.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Shown as income in Income & Expenditure Account
Spread over 5 years in the I&E Account
Capitalised and added to Capital Fund in Balance Sheet
Shown as a liability in Balance Sheet

elimination_hints.hint: Life fees are capital, not revenue. They should not be shown as income in I&E.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating capital receipt as revenue income.

elimination_hints.hint: Spreading is not the standard NCERT treatment. It is capitalised directly.
It is not a liability — it is part of the organisation's own fund (Capital Fund).

question_text: Donation received for a specific purpose (e.g., Building Fund Donation) is treated as:
explanation: Specific donations (earmarked for a particular purpose like building, tournament, etc.) are treated as CAPITAL receipts. They are shown as a separate fund (e.g., Building Fund) on the liabilities side of the Balance Sheet, not as income in I&E Account.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Capital receipt — credited to the specific fund on Balance Sheet
Revenue income — shown in Income & Expenditure Account
Deferred income — spread over multiple years

elimination_hints.hint: Only general (non-specific) donations of small amounts are revenue income.
Spreading is not the standard treatment for specific donations in NPO accounting.
It is a fund/reserve, not a payable. The NPO uses it for the specified purpose.

question_text: The main source of income for a Not-for-Profit Organisation is:
explanation: The main and most regular source of income for most NPOs is subscription from members. Members pay annual subscriptions which form the primary recurring revenue. Other sources include donations, grants, and fees.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Sale of goods is a trading activity — NPOs are not traders.
Commission is earned by agents/brokers, not typical for NPOs.
Interest on capital is a partnership concept, not NPO.

question_text:
Match the following NPO terms:

Column I:
(i) Receipts & Payments Account
(ii) Income & Expenditure Account
(iii) Capital Fund
(iv) Life Membership Fees

Column II:
(P) Equivalent to Profit & Loss Account
(Q) Added to Capital Fund directly
(R) Excess of assets over liabilities
(S) Summary of cash transactions
explanation: R&P Account → Summary of cash transactions (S). I&E Account → Equivalent to P&L (P). Capital Fund → Excess of assets over liabilities (R). Life Membership Fees → Added to Capital Fund directly (Q).
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: R&P is cash summary (S), not P&L equivalent (P). I&E is the P&L equivalent.
I&E is P&L equivalent (P), not excess of assets over liabilities (R). R is Capital Fund.
R&P is cash summary (S), not added to Capital Fund (Q).
